title: End-to-End Molecular Dynamics with a Langevin Thermostat on Quantum Circuits
authors: Masari Watanabe, Hirofumi Nishi, Taichi Kosugi, Shigekazu Hidaka, Ryo Sakurai, Yu-ichiro Matsushita
abstract: We construct a quantum-circuit framework for finite-temperature molecular dynamics in the canonical ensemble (NVT) with a Langevin thermostat, connecting canonical state preparation to subsequent physical-property readouts. The classical nuclear phase-space distribution is encoded as a Koopman--von Neumann (KvN) wave function, and canonical state preparation is formulated as Langevin-type Fokker--Planck relaxation. The Hamiltonian Liouville flow, momentum friction, and momentum diffusion are decomposed into separate circuit blocks. The friction block is represented by a symmetrized momentum-space dilation, whereas the diffusion block is implemented as a cosine filter realized by probabilistic imaginary-time evolution (PITE). We analytically quantify the leading-order temperature bias caused by replacing the Gaussian diffusion kernel with this PITE-realized cosine filter. This analysis yields an internal-temperature correction that targets the desired physical equilibrium distribution. As a proof-of-concept demonstration connecting quantum chemistry to KvN nuclear dynamics, we study the H$_2$ molecule. Numerical simulations show relaxation from a nonequilibrium phase-space distribution to a canonical KvN state. From this canonical state, we demonstrate two complementary readouts: a dynamical quantum-phase-estimation readout of the vibrational density of states associated with the H--H stretch coordinate and a static canonical evaluation of the transition-state-theory (TST) rate constant. This work demonstrates, in a minimal molecular system, a circuit-level protocol that connects Langevin canonical state preparation to physical-property calculations, providing a concrete step toward quantum--classical hybrid molecular dynamics on quantum computers.
summary: This paper develops a quantum-circuit framework to simulate molecular dynamics in the canonical ensemble using a Langevin thermostat. It encodes the classical phase-space density as a Koopman-von Neumann wave function, allowing for the preparation of equilibrium states on a quantum computer. The protocol successfully calculates key physical observables, such as the VDOS and TST rate constant, for the H2 molecule.
main_problem: To construct a quantum-circuit framework for performing finite-temperature molecular dynamics in the canonical ensemble (NVT) using a Langevin thermostat.
main_result: The authors successfully demonstrate an end-to-end quantum protocol that prepares the canonical phase-space state and uses it to calculate both the vibrational density of states (VDOS) and the Transition-State-Theory (TST) rate constant for the H2 molecule.
method: The dynamics are mapped onto a quantum circuit sequence that decomposes the Langevin evolution into Hamiltonian, friction, and diffusion blocks, with the diffusion block implemented via Probabilistic Imaginary-Time Evolution (PITE).
model_or_system: The system is the H2 molecule, modeled using the Born-Oppenheimer approximation. The dynamics are governed by the Langevin equation, whose Fokker-Planck evolution is encoded in the Koopman-von Neumann (KvN) wave function.
key_observables: Vibrational Density of States (VDOS) via Quantum Phase Estimation (QPE) and the Transition-State-Theory (TST) rate constant.
important_parameters: Target physical temperature ($T_{	ext{phys}}$), friction coefficient ($\gamma$), and the time step ($\Delta t$).
main_assumptions: The Born-Oppenheimer approximation is retained, and the quantum simulation requires an analytical correction to account for the leading-order temperature bias introduced by the PITE-realized cosine filter.
figures_summary: Figure 1 summarizes the workflow from electronic structure to VDOS (via QPE) and TST rate constant. Figure 8 compares finite-particle phase-space samples to the analytically encoded canonical KvN density.
paper_structure: The paper details the mathematical derivation of the Langevin dynamics, maps the evolution onto a quantum circuit structure (decomposing the Liouville flow), and then demonstrates the application of this circuit pipeline to calculate both dynamical (VDOS) and static (TST) properties.
why_interesting: This work provides a concrete, circuit-level protocol for quantum-classical hybrid molecular dynamics, directly linking quantum computation to fundamental statistical mechanics problems like canonical ensemble preparation and rate theory.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. End-to-end Koopman–von Neumann (KvN)–Langevin molecular-dynamics pipeline. The H2 electronic structure provides the Born–Oppenheimer potential and Hellmann–Feynman force, which are embedded as an R-dependent KvN force kick. Repeated KvN–Langevin updates prepare a canonical nuclear phase-space state, from which two observables are read out: the Raman-active vibrational density of states by quantum phase estimation and the transition-state-theory rate constant from a canonical flux expectation.

FIG. 2. Quantum-circuit diagram for the many-particle KvN–Langevin one-step evolution. (a) Overall one- step circuit UPITE(σH) UF(∆t) UNVE(∆t) used for canonical state preparation. (b) Implementation of the many-particle UNVE(∆t) block, showing the drift–kick–drift structure based on a second-order symmetric Suzuki–Trotter decomposition, UNVE(∆t) ≃U1(∆t/2) U2(∆t) U1(∆t/2). (c) PITE/cos-filter implementation of UPITE(σH), representing the diffusion block in the momentum Fourier space.

FIG. 3. One-qubit Pauli representation of the H2 elec- tronic structure. (a) Coeﬀicients a(R), b(R), and c(R) in the reduced Hamiltonian ˆHe(R) = a(R)ˆIe +b(R) ˆZe +c(R) ˆ Xe. (b) Ground-state PES relative to its minimum, compared with the FCI/STO-3G reference. This representation supplies V (R) and F(R) for the KvN force kick used in the molecular- dynamics simulations.

FIG. 4. Canonical state preparation and relaxation toward the H2 bond. (a)–(f) Log-scale snapshots of the phase-space density |ψ(R, P, t)|2. An initial wave packet on the dissociation side moves toward the bond-length region and relaxes under the KvN–Langevin thermostat. (g) Mean internuclear distance ⟨R⟩(t). (h) Kinetic temperature Tkin(t), with Tphys = 947 K and the corrected internal temperature Tint = 942 K. (i) Kullback–Leibler divergence DKL(ρ(t)∥ρeq) from the canonical reference distribution.

FIG. 5. LCU+QPE circuit for the VDOS readout. (a) The LCU unitary Uα prepares the branch-selective states |α±⟩∝ˆA± |ψeq⟩with a flag qubit. (b) QPE is applied to |α±⟩using the Hamiltonian KvN time evolution, and the flag and phase ancilla measurements give the branch-resolved fre- quency distributions.
